description: The provided image is an irrelevant software button icon unrelated to the low-density medium Mn steel research. It contains a gray background, a half-blue half-yellow circular ring, a red bookmark-style symbol in the center of the ring, and the text "Check for updates" below the ring. No valid information related to the studied Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C and Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C alloy systems, intercritical annealing processes, microstructure characterization results, mechanical performance data, or other research contents of the paper can be extracted from this image, so no conclusion of the associated study can be supported by this image.

This figure consists of 6 subplots showing thermodynamically predicted equilibrium phase volume fractions as a function of temperature (range 0-1400 °C) for Fe-8Mn-0.2C medium Mn steels with varying alloy additions: (a) 0 wt.% Al, (b) 1 wt.% Al, (c) 2 wt.% Al, (d) 3 wt.% Al, (e) 4 wt.% Al, (f) 3 wt.% Al + 1 wt.% Si. The identified phases from the legend are BCC (ferrite), FCC (austenite), cementite, M23C6 carbide, and liquid phase. The highlighted yellow intercritical temperature (ICT) range starting at 600 °C widens progressively with increasing Al content, from 88 °C (600-688 °C) for 0Al steel to 380 °C (600-980 °C) for 4Al steel. δ-ferrite, an additional BCC phase, appears in the ICT range when Al content reaches 4 wt.%, or when 1 wt.% Si is added to the 3Al steel. These results support the selection of 3Al (δ-ferrite free) and 3Al-1Si (δ-ferrite containing) alloys for subsequent intercritical annealing experiments and mechanical property evaluation.

This figure consists of two subplots showing thermodynamic prediction results for two low-density medium Mn steels, M1 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C, Si-free) and M2 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C, Si-containing). Subplot (a) illustrates the predicted equilibrium austenite fraction (%) as a function of intercritical annealing temperature (IAT, ranging from 600°C to 900°C): both steels show continuously increasing austenite fraction with rising IAT, and at the selected IAT of 700°C, M1 has ~47% austenite while M2 has ~32% austenite. Subplot (b) presents predicted elemental composition (wt.%) in austenite at different temperatures: austenite stabilizers Mn and C show decreasing concentration with rising IAT, while Al and Si concentrations change slightly, with M1 holding slightly higher Mn content than M2 at the same temperature. These results support the selection of 700°C as IAT, where Mn content in austenite reaches ~11 wt.% to ensure sufficient austenite stability against martensitic transformation during post-annealing cooling.

This figure presents the thermo-mechanical processing route for two low-density medium Mn steels (M1: Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C, M2: Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C) alongside corresponding microstructures after each processing step. The plot has temperature (°C, y-axis) and time (hr, x-axis), covering three main steps: 1100 °C homogenization followed by water quenching, 1100 °C hot rolling followed by air cooling, and 700 °C 1-hour intercritical annealing followed by water quenching. Eight micrographs display microstructures at each stage: as-cast M2-AC (200 μm scale), homogenized M1-AC (100 μm) and M2-HQ (500 μm), post-homogenization quenched M1-HQ (100 μm), hot-rolled M1-HR (5 μm) and M2-HR (50 μm), intercritical annealed M1-IA (3 μm) and M2-IA (5 μm). Si addition in M2 induces δ-ferrite formation, while M1 contains no δ-ferrite; intercritical annealing yields higher retained austenite fraction in M1 than M2, verifying that Si addition reduces austenite stability in tested medium Mn steels.

This figure presents SEM micrographs of hot-rolled low-density medium Mn steels and corresponding XRD patterns for phase identification. Subplots a₁-a₂ show the microstructure of M1-HR (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C without Si addition), consisting of polygonal α-ferrite and lath-shaped martensite-austenite (α'+γ) constituents, with scale bars of 5 μm and 3 μm respectively. Subplots b₁-b₃ display the microstructure of M2-HR (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C with 1 wt% Si addition), containing δ-ferrite, α-ferrite and finer α'+γ phases, with scale bars of 50 μm, 5 μm and 500 nm respectively. Subplot c shows XRD patterns of both samples, with 2θ ranging from 40° to 110°, confirming the presence of BCC-structured ferrite/martensite and FCC-structured austenite; the calculated retained austenite fraction is 5.1% for M1-HR and 5.7% for M2-HR. This figure demonstrates that Si addition promotes δ-ferrite formation in hot-rolled medium Mn steels, and both as-hot-rolled samples have low retained austenite content, supporting subsequent analysis of microstructure evolution during intercritical annealing.

This figure presents microstructural characterization of the M1-IA sample, which is an Si-free low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C) subjected to intercritical annealing at 700 °C for 1 h followed by water quenching after hot rolling. Subfigures a₁ to a₄ are secondary electron SEM images at different magnifications with scales of 20 μm, 5 μm, 3 μm, 2 μm respectively, labeling α-ferrite and martensite-austenite (α'+γ) constituent phases. Subfigure b is an EBSD phase map with a 5 μm scale, where orange areas represent austenite and white areas correspond to ferrite. The annealed M1 sample has a dual-phase microstructure of α-ferrite and retained austenite with 45 vol% austenite. SEM images show the morphology of ferrite matrix and dispersed austenite/martensite-austenite islands, while the EBSD map intuitively presents the spatial distribution of austenite. This figure confirms that intercritical annealing produces a multiphase microstructure with high austenite content in M1 steel, supporting that high retained austenite enables excellent TRIP effect and superior mechanical properties.

This figure characterizes the microstructure of M2-IA sample, which is Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C low-density medium Mn steel after intercritical annealing at 700℃ for 1 h followed by water quenching. Subfigures a1 to a4 are scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images with gradually increasing magnification, corresponding to scale bars of 200 μm, 20 μm, 5 μm, and 3 μm respectively, identifying phases including δ-ferrite, α-ferrite, and martensite-austenite (α'+γ) constituent. Subfigure b is an electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) phase map with a scale bar of 5 μm, where the orange region represents austenite and the white region represents ferrite phases. The characterization confirms that the Si-added M2 steel has a tri-phase microstructure of δ-ferrite, α-ferrite, and austenite after intercritical annealing, supporting the conclusion that Si addition promotes δ-ferrite precipitation, adjusts phase composition, and further influences austenite stability in medium Mn steels.

This is an X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern for two intercritically annealed low-density medium Mn steel samples: M1-IA (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C, black curve) and M2-IA (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C, red curve). The x-axis represents 2θ ranging from 40° to 110°, while the y-axis denotes diffraction intensity in arbitrary units (a.u.). Detected diffraction peaks are assigned to BCC α-ferrite (crystal planes 110, 200, 211, 220) and FCC austenite (γ, crystal planes 111, 200, 220, 311, 222). Quantitative phase calculation reveals that M1-IA has an austenite volume fraction (fγ) of 45.6%, while M2-IA has a significantly lower fγ of 19.6%. This result verifies that 1 wt% Si addition reduces retained austenite content after intercritical annealing, consistent with EBSD characterization and thermodynamic predictions, supporting the conclusion that Si facilitates δ-ferrite precipitation and suppresses austenite retention in the studied alloy system.

This figure contains three subsections for two low-density medium Mn steels: M1 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C, no Si addition) and M2 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C, with 1 wt% Si addition). Subplots a₁ and a₂ display calculated room-temperature retained austenite fractions (via Koistinen-Marburger model) and Mₛ temperatures as functions of intercritical annealing (IA) temperature for M1 and M2, respectively: below the critical IA temperature (where Mₛ < room temperature), full retention of equilibrium austenite is expected, while retained austenite fraction decreases with increasing IA temperature above this threshold. Subplot b shows grain size distribution of both steels, indicating M1 has a higher fraction of fine grains (<0.5 μm) than M2, which improves austenite stability. Subplot c is a schematic illustrating elemental partitioning during IA: Mn and C diffuse from α ferrite to austenite in both steels, while Si additionally partitions from austenite to δ and α ferrite in M2. This figure supports that elemental segregation and fine grain effect lead to higher experimental retained austenite fractions than model predictions, and Si addition reduces austenite stability in M2.

This schematic illustrates the evolution of Mn concentration profiles across the interface between austenite (γ, shaded region) and ferrite/δ-ferrite (α/δ) during intercritical annealing of low-density medium Mn steels at 700℃. Subfigure (a) corresponds to the initial state (t=0) when the sample is just heated to annealing temperature, with uniform Mn concentrations in γ (C_Mn^γ) and α/δ (C_Mn^{α/δ}) phases consistent with hot-rolled conditions. Subfigure (b) shows the intermediate state (t=t1), where local equilibrium is established at the phase interface: interfacial Mn concentration increases to C_Mn^{γ(eq)} in γ and decreases to C_Mn^{α/δ(eq)} in α/δ, forming concentration gradients in both phases. Subfigure (c) represents the ideal equilibrium state (t=∞), with homogeneous equilibrium Mn concentrations in both phases, and the γ/α/δ interface moving towards α/δ to expand the γ phase region. This figure demonstrates Mn partitioning from ferrite to austenite during intercritical annealing, which is the key mechanism enhancing austenite stability and adjusting retained austenite fraction for optimized mechanical properties.

This figure combines engineering stress-strain curves and tensile fracture SEM characterizations for two low-density medium Mn steels (M1: Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C, M2: Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C) processed via hot rolling (HR) and intercritical annealing (IA). Subplots a and d display mechanical performance: HR samples (M1-HR, M2-HR) show brittle fracture with high ultimate tensile strength (1712±10 MPa, 1055±15 MPa) and low total elongation (14.8±0.5%, 15.4±0.8%), while IA samples (M1-IA, M2-IA) exhibit ductile behavior with moderate strength (867±10 MPa, 698±20 MPa) and significantly improved elongation (71.8±0.5%, 59.4±0.6%). Subplots b, c, e, f are 10 μm-scale SEM fractographs: HR samples present cleavage features including flat facets and river markings, while IA samples show typical dimple rupture. This figure confirms that intercritical annealing introduces stable retained austenite, enabling excellent strength-ductility matching via TRIP effect, with Si-free M1 achieving a higher product of strength and ductility (61 GPa%) than Si-containing M2 (41 GPa%).

This figure compares the tensile mechanical behavior and microstructure evolution of two intercritically annealed low-density medium Mn steels: M1-IA (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C, Si-free) and M2-IA (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C, Si-added). Subplot a shows true stress and strain hardening rate as functions of true strain: the strain hardening rate of M1-IA reaches 1338 MPa at the end of stage II (point A), while that of M2-IA reaches 989 MPa (point B), with M1-IA exhibiting higher strength and elongation. Subplot b shows the exponential decay of retained austenite volume fraction with true strain, fitted as f_γ=45.6*e^(-3.6ε) for M1-IA and f_γ=19.6*e^(-3.8ε) for M2-IA, indicating higher austenite mechanical stability in M1-IA. Subplots c and d are post-tensile EBSD phase maps (red: ferrite, green: austenite), with retained austenite fractions of 6.5% for M1-IA and 3.2% for M2-IA, confirming the TRIP effect. Subplots e/g and f/h are KAM maps before and after tensile test respectively (KAM range 0 to 5). Significant KAM increase after tension indicates dislocation accumulation and martensite transformation; distinct strain partitioning is observed in M2-IA with low-KAM δ-ferrite and high-KAM transformed regions. This figure confirms that retained austenite volume fraction and stability collectively determine medium Mn steel mechanical performance, and Si addition reduces austenite stability via introducing δ-ferrite.

This schematic illustrates the Transformation Induced Plasticity (TRIP) deformation mechanism in low-density medium Mn steels (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C with/without 1 wt% Si, after intercritical annealing). It shows microstructure evolution with increasing applied strain: initially, three phases exist: γ_c (austenite that directly undergoes TRIP to form α' martensite), α ferrite, and γ_f (austenite that experiences dislocation glide before TRIP transformation, with red arrows representing Mn partitioning from ferrite to austenite). As strain increases, γ_c first transforms to striped α' martensite, while dislocations (marked with ⊥ symbols) accumulate in α ferrite and γ_f. At higher strain, γ_f also transforms to martensite via the TRIP effect. This gradual deformation-induced martensite formation from austenite delivers sustained strain hardening, which accounts for the excellent product of strength and elongation of the studied steels.
